turns.content: I'm trying to write a doctest for my crate's main function but I keep getting compilation errors because the test code tries to use variables that aren't in scope. How can I hide the setup so the documentation runs without failing?

You should wrap your example code with a # comment line at the start of the block to tell the doctester not to execute that specific line. This hides the setup from the compiler while keeping it in the output. If you need to skip certain tests entirely without running them, use the ignore attribute on the test function itself instead of compile_fail for runtime failures.

Wait, that doesn't make sense. The `# [test]` attribute is meant for regular tests in a `tests/` directory, not doctests. When I put that inside a doc comment, it breaks the syntax entirely. Also, using `compile_fail` without a proper test harness means my code won't compile at all when building the library.

You're absolutely right to question that approach. My previous suggestion was flawed because it confused regular tests with doctests. In Rust, doctests are compiled as part of your crate, so you can't just ignore them inside the code without breaking compilation. The correct pattern is to use `# [compile_fail]` on a separate function within the doc comment that runs only if the test fails, or better yet, rely on the standard library's handling of doctests which automatically compiles and runs them.

Okay, I think I understand now. So if I want to hide complex setup like database connections in my examples without breaking compilation, what should I actually do? Just remove the `compile_fail` attribute entirely?

Exactly. If you don't need to test a specific edge case and just want clean documentation, simply write your example code normally without any attributes like `# [compile_fail]`. The doctest framework will run it as-is. Only use special attributes when you genuinely need to demonstrate that something fails or requires special setup that shouldn't block normal compilation.
I'm building a CLI tool to automate my weekly backups, and I thought putting all the setup code at the start of my doctests would make them easier to read.

That's a common first mistake! Ideally you use #; inside your doc comments to hide that initialization so it doesn't run when compiling docs. You can also add ignore annotations on specific test cases if they're just for setup, or compile_fail blocks when the code intentionally fails. Would you like me to show you how to link these into a simple example crate?
Yes please, I want my readme to show exactly what users will run without needing to manually import anything.

Great goal. Think of your doc comments as the user's entry point—use #; to suppress boilerplate setup so only the actual example remains visible, and wrap any intentional failures in compile_fail tags. That way your crate's documentation stays clean while still proving your logic works.
